data.title: Voya Strategic Income Opportunities Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Unconstrained and flexible approach, investing broadly across the global debt markets.

Key takeaways

• Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24.

• The Fund underperformed its benchmark, the ICE BofA USD 3M Deposit Offered Rate Constant Maturity Index (the Index), on net asset value basis.

• Sector allocation along with security selection decisions contributed over the quarter while duration and yield curve decisions detracted over the period, where the rate rally seen in the previous quarter reversed course as inflation concerns and a more cautious U.S. Federal Reserve led to markets repricing yields higher.

• Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation.

Portfolio review

Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24. The gross domestic product (GDP) report for 3Q24 once again came in elevated (initially reporting at 2.8% and later revised to 3.1%) driven largely by strong consumer and government spending, which grew at 3.7% and 5.1%, respectively. Notably, consumer spending accounted for roughly 80% of the overall growth, highlighting its critical role in the economy.

Inflationary pressures remained a key concern. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surprised to the upside in early October, and while subsequent data showed stability in both CPI and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation, both remained elevated. Core PCE, the where it was trending in the summer. Notably, core goods prices, which had experienced a deflationary trend for several months, began to reaccelerate.

Labor market dynamics continued to show signs of softening without entering a state of deterioration. Job gains remained reasonable, and although the unemployment rate resumed its upward trajectory, it remained below the high watermark set earlier in the year. Meanwhile, wage growth remained strong, which helps explains both the resilience of consumer spending and the stickiness of services inflation.

While the Fed continued to deliver rate cuts at both the November and December meetings, the December cut was accompanied by more "hawkish" elements. Specifically, the Fed's Dot plot indicated only two cuts projected for 2025 down from four in the previous iteration. Additionally, officials moved their projection for both growth and inflation higher, while their projection for the unemployment rate moved lower and only slightly above the current level.

Overall, the fourth quarter of 2024 was characterized by resilient labor market dynamics, strong economic growth and sticky inflation. The impact on fixed income performance was mixed, with yields rising and credit spreads tightening, leading to modestly negative total returns for most fixed income sectors.

Sector allocation along with security selection decisions contributed over the quarter while duration and yield curve decisions detracted over the period, where the rate rally seen in the previous quarter reversed course as inflation concerns and a more cautious Fed led to markets repricing yields higher. Our overweight to securitized credit contributed broadly over the period. The non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and credit risk transfers (CRT) sector was the largest contributor, where spreads tightened on positive economic fundamental factors and optimism for future policies. Allocation to corporate credit also contributed, albeit to a lesser extent.

Additionally, positive security selection results in agency mortgages were realized due to our allocation to off-benchmark collateralized mortgage obligations (CMO) which outperformed despite the underperformance of Index constituents.

Current strategy and outlook

Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation. Meanwhile, we expect inflation to resume its downward trend, driven by moderating service prices. In this area however, the election result lowers our conviction as an adverse policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits would sustain higher levels of demand while constraining supply. That said, we believe a negative market reaction would likely compel the administration to temper those policies before they derail growth.

Assuming this is the case, and inflation does not reaccelerate, we expect the Fed will tolerate inflation that is slightly above their target and deliver a few more rate cuts in an effort to preserve the current cycle.

Overall, this backdrop should be favorable for credit sectors, however we continue to favor high-quality, shorter-dated assets given where valuations are. Over the quarter we reduced our allocation to longer dated investment grade corporates and are now positioned with an underweight from a spread duration perspective.

Similarly, we maintain only a modest allocation to high yield corporates, while high quality securitized makes up much of our active risk. If there is a misstep in policy implementation, we believe we are well positioned to capitalize on the short-lived market dislocation and will look to add market risk further out the curve.

An investor should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund(s) contains this and other information, visit us at www.voyainvestments.com or call (800) 992-0180. Please read all materials carefully before investing.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Voya Multi-Manager International Small Cap Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Invests primarily in securities of small market capitalization companies located outside the United States, including emerging markets.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the U.S. Federal Reserve’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 basis points (bp), ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, lead to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Portfolio review

The Fund’s manager target allocations as of December 31, 2024, were 50% to Victory Capital Management, LLC and 50% to Acadian Asset Management, LLC.

Sub-adviser details

Acadian Asset Management, LLC

For the quarter, the Fund’s Acadian Asset Management, LLC (AAMI) sleeve outperformed the S&P Developed ex-U.S. Small Cap Index. Stock selection and country allocations contributed to returns.

Key sources of positive active return included stock selection in both Canada and Japan, and a combination of stock selection and an underweight position in the United Kingdom. Leading advances within these markets respectively included a position in CI Financial Corp., a holding in Konica Minolta Inc., and an investment in JET2 Plc. Detractors included a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in Norway, and stock selection in Denmark and an overweight position in Brazil. Leading declines within these markets in turn included a position in Hoegh Autoliners ASA, a holding in Rockwool AS and an investment in C&AModas S.A.

From a sector perspective, key sources of positive active return included a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in information technology, a combination of stock selection and an underweight position in materials, as well as stock selection in consumer discretionary. Leading advances within these sectors respectively included a position in SimilarWeb Ltd., a holding in Regis Resources Ltd., and an investment in Pandora AS. Lastly, a combination of stock selection and an overweight position in industrials detracted from active return led by a position in Rockwool AS.

Bottom-up stock selection continued to drive the portfolio.

Victory Capital Management, LLC

For the quarter, the Fund’s Victory Capital Management, LLC sleeve outperformed the S&P Developed ex-U.S. Small Cap Index. Security selection was positive as excess returns were generated in three of five regions and seven of the eleven economic sectors. From a style perspective, the Fund’s overall exposure to value, business momentum and quality factors contributed to relative performance, with business momentum generating the strongest signal. At the sector level, notable outperformance was generated in information technology and consumer discretionary. Information technology performance was boosted by a sizable position in Celestica, Inc. which remains a top contributor for the year following stronger-than-expected quarterly results and upgraded guidance. The Canadian electronics manufacturing company remains a beneficiary of hyperscaler spending on data center equipment. Technology One Ltd. was another strong performer. The Australian business software company gained after reporting full-year results which included a steady increase in annual recurring revenue. Within consumer discretionary, Round One Corp., a Japanese operator of indoor leisure facilities, moved higher with positive monthly sales growth alongside continued store expansion optimism in the United States.

On the downside, selection was weakest in materials, with the largest impact coming from two holdings. Swiss chemical company Clariant AG fell with other producers as the demand environment continues to be difficult and restocking expectations have been pushed to later in 2025. Korean defense and copper producer Poongsan Corp. declined as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East appeared to ease and base metals prices experienced a downturn during the quarter. Relative performance also lagged in utilities and was driven down by a position in Boralex Inc., a Canadian renewable energy company with 24% of its capacity in the U.S. The stock declined on concerns of a weaker growth outlook for U.S. renewable energy.

We continue to be guided by our bottom-up analysis and remain focused on stock selection while adhering to our disciplined country and sector risk exposures.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report—percentage of portfolio investments, as of 12/31/2024: CI Financial Corp. (OTCPK:CIXXF) 0.00%, Konica Minolta, Inc. (OTCPK:KNCAF) 0.12%, JET2 Plc. (OTCPK:DRTGF) 0.64%, Hoegh Autoliners ASA 0.05%, Rockwool AS (OTCPK:RKWAD) 0.88%, C&AModas S.A. 0.00%, SimilarWeb Ltd. (SMWB) 0.16%, Regis Resources Ltd. (OTCPK:RGRNF) 0.20%, Pandora AS (OTCPK:PNDZF) 1.35%, Celestica, Inc. (CLS) 0.85%, Technology One Ltd. (OTCPK:THNOF) 0.39%, Round One Corp. (OTCPK:RNDOF) 0.31%, Clariant AG (OTCPK:CLZNF) 0.24%, Poongsan Corp. 0.02%, and Boralex Inc. (OTCPK:BRLXF) 0.16%.

0.00% indicates that the security was not or is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to daily change.

The S&P Developed Ex-U.S. SmallCap Index is an unmanaged index of small-cap stocks from developed countries, excluding the United States. The Index does not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

The MSCI Europe, Australasia and Far East (EAFE) Small Cap Index is an unmanaged index which measures the performance of small capitalization equities among developed markets around the world, excluding the United States and Canada. The Index does not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. You could lose money on your investment and any of the following risks, among others, could affect investment performance. The following principal risks are presented in alphabetical order which does not imply order of importance or likelihood: China Investing Risks – Investing through Stock Connect; Company; Convertible Securities; Credit; Currency; Derivative Instruments; Environmental, Social, and Governance (Equity); Environmental, Social, and Governance (Multi-Manager); Environmental, Social, and Governance (Qualitative); Foreign (Non-U.S.) Investments/ Developing and Emerging Markets; Index Strategy (Fund); Growth Investing; Interest Rate; Investment Model; Liquidity; Market; Market Disruption and Geopolitical; Other Investment Companies; Real Estate Companies and Real Estate Investment Trusts; Securities Lending; Small-Capitalization Company; Value Investing. Investors should consult the Fund’s Prospectus and Statement of Additional Information for a more detailed discussion of the Fund’s risks.

An investment in the Fund is not a bank deposit and is not insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, the Federal Reserve Board or any other government agency.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management’s current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may differ materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Portfolio holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors.

The Fund discussed may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance to vary from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information.

©2024 Voya Investments Distributor, LLC • 230 Park Ave, New York, NY 10169 • All rights reserved.

Original Post

data.title: Vertiv Holdings: Time To Be Greedy
data.description:
About Vertiv Holdings

As you may know, Vertiv Holdings Co (NYSE:VRT) is now a $24-billion market cap firm that develops, manufactures, and maintains key digital infrastructure tech and lifecycle solutions for data centers, communications networks, and commercial and industrial applications worldwide (based on its SEC filings business description). They offer products ranging from power management products, thermal management products, integrable rack solutions, and monitoring devices for providing services such as e-commerce, online banking, and IoT. Vertiv sells products across brands - including Vertiv, Liebert, and Avocent - to sectors such as cloud services, healthcare, and financial services.

Investment Thesis Update

Lat time I upgraded the stock to "Buy", I managed to catch the exact local peak in VRT, which is always frustrating. But as time went on and the market's turmoil exacerbated further, the stock's price dipped too deep to explain logically how tariffs can break down its growth story. I mean, I'm sure the firm will feel the negative impact of the updated policies, but Vertiv Holdings seems to be well-protected and ready to weather the storm with limited harm. The stock has finally become too cheap and tempting to ignore. That's why I believe it's a great opportunity for investors to lower their average cost in it while they can.

Why Do I Think So?

Vertiv closed out its FY2024 with Q4 revenues at $2.35 billion (+25.7% YoY), beating the consensus estimate by 8.87% - the largest quarterly beat over the past few years, according to Seeking Alpha data. What's notable here: VRT's op-line strength seems to be broad-based, with organic sales growth exceeding 20% in both the Americas and APAC, and surging >31% in the EMEA region. This says a lot about the firm's diversification, although over 53% of its sales still come from the US (the mix is shifting slowly outside the US with a 117 bps reduction on a YoY basis). The operating profit margin is also expanding rapidly: In Q4 we saw Americas' margin hitting 25.6% (+420 bps YoY) and EMEA's margin showing a similar absolute figure, while in APAC, the margin is still lagging.

Anyway, the firm's consolidated adjusted EBIT reached ~$504 million (+53% YoY) with a consolidated margin of ~21.5% (+380 basis points YoY), exceeding guidance by 110 basis points. As a result, the quarter's EPS of $0.99 showed a 77% YoY increase and beat the analysts' consensus estimates of $0.82 with a healthy margin (it wasn't the biggest beat as with revenues, but still - a 20% beat seems massive to me).

To smooth out the seasonality and look at the full-year results, we see that Q4 was basically a continuation of the growth that started well before 2024.

The rise in profitability doesn't come just on paper as for FY2024, VRT's adjusted FCF went up by 46% to over $1.1 billion, allowing Vertiv to reduce its net leverage to 1x by year-end. The firm now holds $1,257.9 million in the cash & ST securities account, based on Seeking Alpha data, which is ~54% more than as of Q4 FY2023 while the net debt item has shrunk by 18% YoY.

As modern data centers require substantially more power and more sophisticated thermal management solutions, Vertiv's offerings get the boost on the demand side, as this is exactly what VRT is capable of providing. According to MarketsAndMarkets' study, the data center liquid cooling market size should hit almost $30 billion in size in about 5 years, growing at a CAGR of ~12.8% annually. And I think that Vertiv's market position may actually win from the recently announced tariffs because over 36% (8 out of 22) of all its manufacturing facilities are already in the Americas:

That's why I believe the CFO's commentary. When asked about the tariffs during the latest earnings call, David Fallon stated that the company's guidance for FY2025 "doesn't contemplate significant impacts from potential new or proposed U.S. tariff policies, with the exception of existing incremental direct tariffs on imports from China", which they assess as having a "relatively immaterial impact" on their financials currently.

We have multiple sources of supply to deal with geographical uncertainty. We believe price cost will be positive for 2025. While questions remain relative to potential tariff impacts, we have been adding regional sourcing and manufacturing options to complement our existing global supply chain.

The situation with tariffs remains very fluid, so it would be premature to discuss it in detail. At the same time, it is worth mentioning we have of course built scenarios and playbooks aimed at strategically mitigating some of the tariff impacts. To be clear, although there is much uncertainty regarding the scope and breadth of tariffs, we believe we are well prepared to take strategic actions to help mitigate risks.

So what's in their guidance for FY2025?

The FY2025 net sales are projected to be ~$9.2 billion at the midpoint (organic growth of ~16%), which is ~$75 million higher than the implied guidance from November 2024 - that's despite absorbing "an estimated incremental $125 million foreign exchange headwind." So again - on an apples-to-apples basis, comparing to the expected 2024 baseline from November - the organic sales outlook is effectively up $200 million. In addition, the adjusted EPS for FY2025 is targeted at $3.55 (the midpoint), which is going to be a 25% increase YoY. The operating leverage should work in the company's favor as the interest expenses keep shrinking and the EBIT margin keeps rising higher with a better sales mix (from my understanding). The firm's adjusted FCF is projected to reach $1.3 billion at the midpoint. So that's why I believe FY2025 should be another great year for their capital allocation practices. I mean, in FY2024, the $1.1 billion in adjusted FCF they generated enabled ~$600 million in share repurchases and a 50% dividend increase, and so this year with ~18% more in FCF, the repurchases should logically be bigger.

Particularly for Q1 FY2025, Vertiv's management expects sales of ~$1.925 billion (implying 19% YoY organic growth) and adjusted EPS of $0.6 (up roughly 40% YoY at the midpoint). While the Q1 EPS guidance midpoint was slightly below the $0.64 consensus initial pre-release estimate (Seeking Alpha data), this way adding to the stock price dip, the management added context, noting that "Q1 2025 sales as a percentage of the full-year guide are actually higher than the comparable figure in 2024". So it suggests to me a rather strong start instead of just an unusual weakness.

Anyway, the current EPS and revenue consensus aligns with the mid-ranges of the updated guidance as far as I can see (the Wall Street analysts even price in a slight premium).

I believe the firm is very likely to beat the current consensus again for FY2025, although I'm not sure the magnitude of this prospective beat would be as strong as we saw last year. Instead of FY2024's 5.82% positive surprise, I think we'll see something close to a 2-3% beat for FY2025. So this assumption leads me to $3.649/share in earnings out-of-consensus expectation for FY2025 (+28% YoY), implying an FWD P/E ratio of ~17.2x. So effectively, the PEG ratio on a forwarding basis is ~0.615x, and to be called "fairly valued" for a growth stock under Peter Lynch's rule, VRT stock price should expand by 62.6% if my math is correct. That implies a fair value per share of $102 and leads me to another "Buy" rating.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

I read in other articles that today's concerns mainly center on the sustainability of Vertiv's order growth - particularly given the flat Q4 result and the slowdown in the EMEA region. It seems to me that the market seeks consistent evidence that Vertiv is capturing its share of the accelerating AI infrastructure spend - until it gets evidence from the updated financials (and probably once again raised guidance), the sentiment and narrative regarding Vertiv Holding specifically may not change. This is the risk investors take buying the dip right away.

Another risk comes from the tariffs. While the management tried to make it clear that the firm wasn't going to feel a major negative impact from tariffs, we can't be 100% sure. The market's sentiment shift is still in full swing after the epic rise we experienced during the post-COVID period, and the higher volatility may hurt growth investors' portfolios (maybe for a few additional years, nobody knows).

The Bottom Line

I know that there's always a risk in situations like today to buy a falling knife, but I believe that VRT isn't one. At least, I see limited impact from the tariffs, and hence don't view the current correction as something that shall be persistent and driven by fundamentals. Yes, the data centers' infrastructure growth may not be as sky-high as expected previously, but it doesn't cancel out VRT's growth prospects. Only if the FY2025 EPS takes a 5% hit, the stock's fair value would be close to today's price (at a 5% discount to the EPS, the implied PEG is close to ~1). But I think we'll likely see a beat to the current consensus, so there's a massive upside potential from here. I reiterate my "Buy" rating.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Evergy: Still A Quality Utility To Buy Now
data.description:
In dividend growth investing, I would argue that consistency is what matters the most. When I either buy a stock for my portfolio or cover it as an analyst, I have to be fairly certain that it can continue to send gradually higher dividend payments to its shareholders (or at least stable payments in ultra-high-yielding situations). Otherwise, I'm not going to buy it or cover it.

As I have detailed in the past, this is what makes regulated utilities so compelling to me. This sector benefits from services with demand that can endure in basically any economic or operating environment. As the economies of underlying service territories grow, so too should a regulated utility's earnings (barring very unfavorable rate case decisions).

One example of a qualitative regulated utility is Evergy, Inc. (NASDAQ:EVRG). When I last covered it with a buy rating in November with Brad Thomas, I was encouraged by the economic development driving its higher capital spending. I also liked the company's investment-grade balance sheet. Shares were also discounted at the time.

Today, I'm maintaining my buy rating. EVRG's five-year capital spending plan is now even higher. The company still has the financial prowess to fund its growth ambitions. Shares are also undervalued enough that I believe sufficient total returns are likely for the foreseeable future.

The Growth Profile Remains Intact

On February 27th, EVRG shared its financial results for the fourth quarter ended December 31st, 2024. The company's operating revenue grew by 5.9% over the year-ago period to $1.26 billion during the quarter. That was nearly $15 million above the Seeking Alpha analyst consensus in the quarter. This can be attributed to the economic development in the area that led to growth in the electric load.

Moving to the bottom line, EVRG's adjusted EPS surged 29.6% higher year-over-year to $0.35 for the fourth quarter. That was $0.02 shy of the analyst consensus. Disciplined cost management led the company's non-GAAP profit margin to expand by about 130 basis points to 6.4% during the quarter. This is how adjusted EPS growth outpaced operating revenue growth in the quarter.

Looking ahead, EVRG has plenty of growth potential on the horizon.

Legislatively, EVRG also looks positioned to benefit from a recent development. According to Chairman and CEO David Campbell's remarks during the Q4 2024 Earnings Call, the company introduced House Bill 2107 in Kansas this year. This would require potential lawsuits to prove that a wildfire was caused by utility negligence. The day before the earnings call, this bill passed unanimously out of committee and was recommended for passage in the House.

EVRG plans to spend $17.5 billion on infrastructure investments from 2025 through 2029. That's up $1.3 billion or 8% over the previously projected capital investment amount.

Aside from EVRG's data center agreements with Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), the company anticipates it will add another 355 megawatts to its electric load. That's going to be achieved through additional economic development in its service territories.

Overall, EVRG expects annual load growth of between 2% to 3% through 2029. This is why it is investing so significantly over the medium term. That also explains how the company expects its rate base to rise by 8.5% annually from $19.5 billion in 2024 to between $29 billion and $30 billion in 2029. Overall, this is the math behind how EVRG believes it can grow its adjusted EPS by the top half of its 4% to 6% range annually through 2029.

Analysts are also convinced that this plan is viable. The FAST Graphs consensus is that adjusted EPS will increase by 5.6% in 2025 to $4.03. Another 5.8% rise in adjusted EPS to $4.26 is being predicted for 2026. In 2027, an additional 5.9% jump in adjusted EPS to $4.51 is the consensus.

That's explained by the fact that the majority of EVRG's $21.1 billion in combined capex and projected dividends is expected to be funded by operating cash flow. The remaining $8.6 billion is slated to come from a mix of $5.8 billion in incremental debt offerings and $2.8 billion in equity issuances.

Financially, EVRG should have the means to take on this additional debt. This is backed up by an interest coverage ratio that was 2.6 in 2024. For a regulated utility, this is adequate (unless otherwise hyperlinked or sourced, all info in this subhead was according to EVRG's Q4 2024 Earnings Press Release and EVRG's Q4 2024 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value Could Be Above $70 A Share

In the nearly five months that have elapsed since my previous article, EVRG has held up remarkably well. Shares have logged a 5% total return as the S&P 500 index (SP500) has fallen 13%. Yet, after this rally, EVRG remains about as appealing of a value now as it was in November.

Shares are trading at a current-year P/E ratio of 16.2. That's moderately below the 10-year normal P/E ratio of 19.5 per FAST Graphs (slightly above the previous 19.3 P/E ratio as FAST Graphs incorporated the most recent year of data into the averages).

Moving forward, I still believe that fair value is one standard deviation below the 10-year average P/E ratio - - or 17.5. This is because EVRG's 5.8% annual adjusted EPS growth consensus is comparable to the 10-year rate of 5%.

The reason for my fair value being a bit lower than the 10-year average has to do with the yield of United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y). Over this time, the average has been 2.5%, according to Macrotrends. The current yield of 4.2% is well above that rate. In the years ahead, I suspect that the 10-year yield will come down 50 to 75 basis points. Assuming an approximately 200 basis point spread (to account for equity risk), that works out to a fair value P/E ratio of 17.5.

As things stand, the calendar year 2025 is 27% complete. This means another 73% of 2025 and 27% of 2026 is yet to come in the next 12 months. That produces a forward 12-month adjusted EPS input of $4.09.

Using this adjusted EPS input with my fair value P/E ratio of 17.5, I arrive at a fair value of $72 a share. Relative to the current $65 share price (as of April 7th, 2025), this equates to a 9% discount to fair value. If EVRG matches the growth consensus and reverts to fair value, it could generate a 31% cumulative total return through 2027.

On The Path To Becoming A Dividend Aristocrat

EVRG's 4.1% forward dividend yield registers above the utility sector median forward yield of 3.4%. That warrants respective B+ and B grades from Seeking Alpha's Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield.

EVRG has also proven itself to be a reliable dividend grower. Over the past five years, EVRG has compounded its dividend by 6% annually. That's 100 basis points above the sector median of 5% and enough for the Quant System to award a B grade for overall dividend growth.

Moving forward, EVRG should have the means to continue handing out mid-single-digit annual dividend growth. This is because the utility's adjusted EPS payout ratio is slated to be in the mid-60% range in 2025. That earns EVRG a B- grade for overall dividend safety from the Quant System.

As a result, I believe that it's likely the utility will build on its 21-year dividend growth streak. This is double the sector median of 10.5 years and puts EVRG on track to become a Dividend Aristocrat by the end of this decade. That's why the Quant System issues an A+ grade for overall dividend consistency.

Risks To Consider

EVRG's fundamentals look to be holding up. However, the company is still vulnerable to risks that could hurt the investment thesis.

One risk to EVRG is that its status as a major utility subjects it to attempted cyber breaches. If any are successful on a wide enough scale, customer data could be jeopardized. That could lead to lawsuits being brought forth against it by customers and fines from regulators.

Another risk to EVRG is the potential that President Trump's reciprocal tariffs could simultaneously harm economic growth and spike inflation. If this happens, utilities could have a difficult time passing on higher financing costs to consumers. That's because regulators may be less willing to approve higher rates in times of economic contraction. In a worst-case scenario, that could force EVRG to absorb those higher costs, harming profit margins in the process.

One final risk to the utility is that the company is entirely based in Kansas and Missouri. If these two markets don't keep growing their economies and populations, that could weigh on EVRG's fundamentals. Separately, an adverse rate case outcome in either of these states could harm its growth potential and lead to credit rating downgrades from rating agencies.

Major natural disasters like tornadoes and windstorms could also harm EVRG's infrastructure more than its commercial insurance coverage. That could shrink the company's earnings power.

Summary: A Solid Dividend Grower For The Long Run

EVRG is a dependable dividend growth stock. The company's significant capital investments planned over the medium term provide a path to generate the rate base growth needed to reach its adjusted EPS growth target. EVRG's financial health is also acceptable. Finally, shares are trading below my fair value estimate. This is why I still expect double-digit annual total returns over the next few years, and I am reiterating my buy rating.

data.title: Voya Securitized Credit Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Invests in fixed income sectors collateralized by distinct asset types: commercial real estate (CMBS), residential housing (RMBS), non-mortgage assets (ABS) and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs).

Portfolio review

Strong economic data continued to come through in the final quarter of 2024, which had the impact of driving spreads tighter while reversing the rate rally experienced in 3Q24.

The gross domestic product (GDP) report for 3Q24 once again came in elevated (initially reporting at 2.8% and later revised to 3.1%) driven largely by strong consumer and government spending, which grew at 3.7% and 5.1%, respectively. Notably, consumer spending accounted for roughly 80% of the overall growth, highlighting its critical role in the economy.

Inflationary pressures remained a key concern. The Consumer Price Index (CPI) surprised to the upside in early October, and while subsequent data showed stability in both CPI and Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation, both remained elevated. Core PCE, the U.S. Federal Reserve's preferred measure of inflation, finished the quarter at 2.8% year-over-year, slightly above where it was trending in the summer. Notably, core goods prices, which had experienced a deflationary trend for several months, began to reaccelerate.

Labor market dynamics continued to show signs of softening without entering a state of deterioration. Job gains remained reasonable, and although the unemployment rate resumed its upward trajectory, it remained below the high watermark set earlier in the year. Meanwhile, wage growth remained strong, which helps explains both the resilience of consumer spending and the stickiness of services inflation.

While the Fed continued to deliver rate cuts at both the November and December meetings, the December cut was accompanied by more "hawkish" elements. Specifically, the Fed's Dot plot indicated only two cuts projected for 2025 down from four in the previous iteration. Additionally, officials moved their projection for both growth and inflation higher, while their projection for the unemployment rate moved lower and only slightly above the current level.

Overall, the fourth quarter of 2024 was characterized by resilient labor market dynamics, strong economic growth and sticky inflation. The impact on fixed income performance was mixed, with yields rising and credit spreads tightening, leading to modestly negative total returns for most fixed income sectors.

For the quarter, the Voya Securitized Credit Fund outperformed the Index on a NAV basis. Sector allocation, security selection, duration and yield curve positions all contributed. Our structural bias away from agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) in favor of credit sectors broadly contributed. Our allocation to non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and credit risk transfer (CRT) was the largest individual contributor as strong borrower characteristics continued to drive spreads tighter. Our overweight to commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) was also a strong contributor, as fundamental factors improved, and the sector continued to benefit from an active primary market. Similarly, positive security selection results in CMBS were realized due to our bias towards higher yielding tranches. Meanwhile, asset-backed securities (ABS) spreads continued to tighten in sympathy with other high quality spread sectors, and as a result, our overweight contributed. Finally, due to our shorter duration profile relative to the Index, the move higher in rates had a positive impact on relative performance.

Current strategy and outlook

Looking forward, we continue to expect economic growth will remain resilient. The recent election outcome further strengthens this view due to election optimism and productivity gains from deregulation. Meanwhile, we expect inflation to resume its downward trend, driven by moderating service prices. In this area, however, the election result lowers our conviction as an adverse policy mix of deficit spending, tariffs and stricter immigration limits would sustain higher levels of demand while constraining supply. That said, we believe a negative market reaction would likely compel the administration to temper those policies before they derail growth.

Assuming this is the case, and inflation does not reaccelerate, we expect the Fed will tolerate inflation that is slightly above their target and deliver a few more rate cuts in an effort to preserve the current cycle.

Overall, this backdrop should be favorable for credit broadly, however, we continue to favor sectors backed by consumer credit and commercial real estate, which are earlier in their cycles relative to corporate credit. As of quarter end, non-agency RMBS and CRT represented our top allocation, followed closely by CMBS. Meanwhile, our allocation to collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) remains relatively low and more concentrated in higher quality (more than A rated) tranches. If there is a misstep in policy implementation, we believe we are well positioned to capitalize on the short-lived market dislocation and will look to add market risk further out the curve. Finally, in response to the move higher in rates, we increased the fund's duration in order to reduce some of our underweight relative to the benchmark.

The Bloomberg U.S. Securitized Index includes the MBS, ABS, and CMBS sectors of the Bloomberg Aggregate universe. Securities prices used to value the benchmark index for the purposes of calculating total return may or may not differ significantly from those used to value securities held within composite portfolios. Index returns do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. You could lose money on your investment and any of the following risks, among others, could affect investment performance. The following principal risks are presented in alphabetical order which does not imply order of importance or likelihood: Credit; Credit Default Swaps; Currency; Derivative Instruments; Environmental, Social, and Governance (Fixed Income); Foreign (Non-U.S.) Investments/ Developing and Emerging Markets; High-Yield Securities; Interest in Loans; Interest Rate; Liquidity; Market Disruption and Geopolitical; Mortgage- and/or Asset-Backed Securities; Other Investment Companies; Prepayment and Extension; Securities Lending; Sovereign Debt; U.S. Government Securities and Obligations; When-Issued, Delayed Delivery and Forward Commitment Transactions. Investors should consult the Fund's prospectus and statement of additional information for a more detailed discussion of the Fund's risks.

The Fund discussed may be available to you as part of your employer sponsored retirement plan. There may be additional plan level fees resulting in personal performance to vary from stated performance. Please call your benefits office for more information.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an offer to sell or solicitation of an offer to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change.

Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management's current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to differ materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may differ materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Portfolio holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors. Past Performance does not guarantee future results.

data.title: ANGL: A Good Product, But Not The Right Time To Buy It
data.description:
Introduction

Fallen Angel Investing has been made popular by Howard Marks, the co-founder of Oaktree Capital Management. The idea is to buy bonds just ranked down from investment grade status to achieve higher returns. Additionally, this comes with the premise that the market will likely overreact on the news of the downgrade and can, therefore, be seen as a contrarian approach. In this article, I will show why Fallen Angel Investing by buying VanEck Fallen Angel High Yield Bond ETF (NASDAQ:ANGL) might be a good idea during more calm times, but not now.

ETF Key Data

ANGL tracks the ICE Fallen Angel High Yield 10% Index and currently yields 6.27% at an expense ratio of 0.25%, making the ETF relatively cheap. At the same time, liquidity for the ETF is high at a daily share volume of almost 1 million. When looking at the top 10 holdings in Figure 1, one can also see that the fund is well diversified, with the top 10 positions only making up 21.87% of the portfolio. Also, as was to be expected, the fund has more than 99% of its bond holdings in corporate bonds. Figure 2 shows the fundamentals of ANGL. At almost 10 years to maturity, the bonds are relatively risky. However, the effective duration (interest rate risk) is only at 4.5 years. Lastly, Figure 3 shows the different bond ratings of ANGL's holdings. As expected, most of the bonds are BB-rated (70%). Nevertheless, there are also some C-rated and even non-rated bonds in the portfolio.

Is ANGL a Good Investment?

Historically speaking, this question can be answered with a yes. Figure 4 shows the performance of ANGL in contrast to other bond strategies, including long-dated treasuries, high-yield bonds, and investment-grade bonds. As can be seen, ANGL has the highest annualized return of all the different bond strategies. Nevertheless, it also has one of the highest risks at a standard deviation of 20.4% and a max. Drawdown of more than 37.2%. In the overall risk-adjusted Sharpe ratio, the ETF nevertheless scored the best. While all of this sounds good at first, there is one thing that has drastically changed over the past few years: interest rates have risen again. This might change everything, as treasury bonds are suddenly worth buying again.

Are Treasuries a Better Buy at the Moment?

Treasuries are often considered riskless, since the US has never gone bankrupt before. Meanwhile, corporate bonds do contain a risk, especially during credit crises like the 2008 crash. Figure 5 shows the yield spread between corporate and treasury bonds. As can be seen, it is historically very low at a difference of less than 2%. This suggests that the return investors currently get in corporate bonds is very low. While ANGL holds slightly riskier bonds than displayed in the graph, this key finding is universal for all corporate bonds and can also be seen at ANGL yielding less than 3% more than iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF).

Risks

One risk for ANGL is, of course, the interest rate risk. While the effective duration is only at 4.5 years, higher interest rates could still mean some losses. While lower interest rates could be beneficial, longer-term bonds would be able to profit more from it. Another factor to consider is valuation. As was already shown in Figure 5, corporate bonds are currently valued as if we are in the best-case scenario (low premium to treasuries), however, there currently is a lot of uncertainty in the market. Trump's tariffs could drastically increase inflation, while a new credit crisis could be on the way, with US delinquency rates being higher than in 2008.

Conclusion

While ANGL is a good product that has produced great risk-adjusted returns in the past, the market's premium to treasury bonds is currently simply too low. This makes buying FALN not worth the risk of increased geopolitical risk and a new credit crisis. For me, the ETF is currently a “hold” as the product is good, while the time to buy it is rather bad.

data.title: Apple Is Falling Far From The Tree: Downgrading To Sell Until Further Notice
data.description:
Apple Inc.’s (NASDAQ:AAPL) (TSX:AAPL:CA) business model is taking a big hit with the U.S.-China trade war, and the situation doesn’t look promising for the market at large. Around 90% of iPhones are assembled in China by Apple maker Hon Hai Precision Industry, which closed down 10% lower on Tuesday. I think the company, and as a result, the stock, will both suffer until the uncertainty around the tariffs subsides or both countries reach a middle ground. With how things are unraveling now, my guess is that won’t happen overnight, especially after China raised U.S. tariffs from 34% to 84% on Wednesday, coming in effect on April 10th, in reaction to President Donald Trump's moves on Tuesday.

Tuesday at midnight, the U.S. additional 50% tariff on China went live, bringing the total to 104% on Chinese goods and products.

See, every year, the market waits for the new iPhone in September for an uptick in demand. Last year’s iPhone 16 didn’t give us that, though the price was more on the cheaper side, reflecting a weak phone market altogether. The story is bigger than Apple, and a slower pace of recovery for smartphone demand is what we’re dealing with. The tariffs will hamper the entire pace of recovery for phones, in my opinion, adding oil to the fire. So, while yes, the market has priced in the tariff news, it hasn’t priced in all that’s going to come after in terms of pressure on margins as Apple tries to maneuver higher prices. While we’re on prices, Apple has pricing power in the U.S. and has the biggest market share. The latter grows sequentially and year over year from 62% in Q4 '23 and 53% in Q3 '24 to 65% in Q4 '24.

Come to think of the economic situation, the rate cuts, and the consumer spending power, are people going to upgrade their iPhones once Apple releases the new iPhone? Maybe some, but I’d say priority will go to everyday items.

From here, I’ll say I don’t think the stock reaction to the tariffs is unwarranted, nor is it an overreaction. And the “dip”? It's not a buying opportunity, either.

Stock price and valuation:

The stock closed down around 5% on Tuesday as a result of the news and is now trading at $172 per share, down from $189 at market open. The stock is down over 22% over the past five days as investors grapple to price in the tariffs. As of now, I don’t think the worst has been priced in yet.

The stock is down around 31% year to date, around double that of the S&P 500 (SP500), down 15.2% during the same period. I’m not pessimistic about Apple for the long term; I think the company will do just fine. Near-term pain is coming, though, and I see more downside ahead before Apple is a buy again.

I upgraded Apple to a strong buyback in early March on the basis that the market priced in 1. The China problem and the competition there, the $500 billion U.S. investment concern, the innovation issue, and the Siri delay. While all that is priced in, and while I remain optimistic about Apple’s upcoming software overhauls to reignite sales growth, I think the company has some near-term downside, and bigger fish to fry.

The P/E ratio has been down over 30.7% year to date, and the PEG ratio has also been down over 28% during the same period. In comparison to the sector median, the valuation metrics lead me to think the stock is expensive at current levels and could have some more downsides ahead. Apple’s forward price/sales are at 6.3, almost triple that of the sector median at 2.1, and price/book is at 44.6, compared to the sector median at 3. The revenue growth is also lagging at 3%, below the sector median of 5%, while negative EPS growth doesn’t make the situation any better.

On the price movement, the stock is currently trading at $172, under all its exponential moving averages, with EMA21 at $211, EMA50 at $222, and EMA200 at $222.4. The stock also broke through prior support levels and reached a low of around $169, which shows weakened momentum, as the RSI is also well beyond the oversold threshold of 30 and currently sits at around 20. While an RSI this low sometimes hints at a bounce from a technical perspective, I think the overall market uncertainty and macro headwinds look like they’ll persist some more, and with no clear catalyst ahead, I’m not too optimistic about a turnaround.

Apple’s production lines: diversification attempt

Before I get into it, here’s a refresher on where Apple makes the bulk of its revenue. It should be no surprise to you when I focus on iPhones, as Apple makes up over 56% of revenue through iPhone sales, 7% from Mac, iPads at 6.5%, while wearables, home, and accessories at 9.4%, and services at 21.2%. Over the past few years, Apple has been trying to diversify its production from China to India, Vietnam, and Malaysia. It all started back during COVID-19 when the supply chain saw some hindrances and chip shortages due to overdependence on China for production. And through the company’s strategy, Apple will see less pain than it would’ve in 2020, but a lot of pain regardless as all countries rush to make a deal, with tariffs on India at 26%, Vietnam at 46%, and Malaysia at 24%, Japan at 24%, South Korea at 25%, and Taiwan at 32%. According to current Woodring estimates, Apple might have to raise prices across its product lines by 17% to 18% across the U.S., where it makes 42% of its revenue. According to Wedbush analyst Dan Ives, “It would take 3 years and $30 billion dollars in our estimation to move even 10% of its supply chain from Asia to the U.S.”

As of now, around 80% of Apple's production capacity is in China, with around 90% of iPhones, 55% of Mac products, and 80% of iPads assembled in China. Currently, around 10% to 15% of iPhones are assembled in India, and while estimates point towards 15% to 20% of overall production happening in India by the end of the year, that’s not nearly enough to offset the damage from the China dependency. The company increased production in Vietnam over the past few years and currently makes around 20% of its iPads there and 90% of its wearable product assembly, like the Apple Watch, according to Evercore ISI. Malaysia and Thailand are growing locations for Mac production as well, and Japan, Taiwan, and Korea are where the company sources components. All we have in the U.S. now is 1. the promised $500 billion investment and the new AI server factory in Texas and 2. Mac Pro is produced in Texas.

I must admit, the five flights full of iPhones that Apple flew to the U.S. during the final week of March, shielded the company from raising prices temporarily. The market got excited about that in pre-market trading on Wednesday, with the stock up over 2.5%, but quickly digested that, and the stock is now down around 2% in pre-market trading following China's retaliation.

What’s next?

India is now Apple’s savior, and the 78% additional tariffs China has over India is proof of that. But a supply chain so complex and embedded in China isn’t going to move to India completely overnight. It would take a few years at best. Apple is currently on track to make 25 million iPhones in India this year, with 10 million for the local market, according to Bank of America analyst Wamsi Mohan. Still, if the company decides to import all 25 million iPhones to the U.S., that would satisfy around 50% of demand in the U.S.

And when it comes to production happening in the U.S., which is Trump’s main goal with the tariffs' situation, I think the country is far too behind on manufacturing power.

Unfortunately, the President's recent tweet doesn’t take into account all the time and capital it takes to move production to the U.S.

Tell me: who will build your iPhones in the U.S.? And what's going to make iPhones cost less than triple their current price if that happens?

data.title: BIZD: Would Go For A Constituent Part
data.description:
The VanEck BDC Income ETF (NYSEARCA:BIZD) is an ETF focused on the set of business development equities that are purchasable in the US. It is redundant in our view to have an ETF of business development stocks because business development stocks are already as diversified of a portfolio as you can get of business development loans. Therefore, any additional expenses paid at the ETF level are unneeded. Otherwise, in terms of where their unrated credit will typically lie in the rating scale and associated yields on the portfolio, there is nothing untoward about their valuation relative to current yields. The question then is around current yields and how credit spreads may move in general. Credit spreads finally reflect more appropriately the economic reality, where in our ETF coverage we've been complaining that credit spreads at historical lows represent wishful thinking by markets despite several outstanding questions, namely around inflation and inflation expectations. But as the threats escalate of a trade war it's necessary to take seriously that risks could rise even further. We are still off Ukraine war levels (July 2022 when realisation really set into markets of the supply chain impacts) of credit spreads by about 200 bps for the higher range of where BIZD constituent loans could be reasonably rated. The recent declines are no surprise and when macro questions come into the picture BIZD is by no means a safe vehicle.

BIZD Quick Breakdown

BIZD contains substantial skew in the securities of business development vehicles listed in US markets. These vehicles make loans to small and medium-sized businesses in the US on a private and unrated basis. Emblematic of these exposures is the Ares Capital Corp (ARCC) which has a 20% allocation within BIZD and can be used as a font of data and a barometer for the sector's general conditions.

As of latest earnings:

  • Around 10% YTMs in the portfolio.
  • Implied credit rating below B but above CCC. Yields as of the report were around 4.5% for the 5-year, which is a reasonably representative horizon for BIZD loans. Implied credit spreads were around 3% for B at the time and 7.4% for CCC. Therefore, the implied rating at 10% yield is somewhere between B and CCC but towards CCC. While BIZD loans are unrated we think this is reasonable, and we don't have a relative valuation issue.

Declines in the ETF as of recently come down to the changes in credit spread. Looking at B, we are about 200 bps away from July 2022 levels and around 200 bps above the December 2024 levels. For CCC, we are 400 bps above December 2024 levels and only 100 bps away from July 2022 levels. We are now seeing another retaliation from China following the successive punitive rate by Trump bringing the tariff on Chinese goods above 100%. In other words, trade cannons are being leveled between the two biggest economies and D-Day is tonight/tomorrow, when these tariffs are meant to take effect. The trade war risks are considerable, and their impacts on the supply chain will be comparable to the Ukraine war, except now the US is at the centre of it where the European proximity to Russia and Ukraine means the locus of disruption was far from US shores, and had some oblique benefits for the US gas economy. In other words, this may very well be worse than the Ukraine war for the US economy.

The only offset is that risk-free rates have fallen YTD, reflecting economic concerns but also possibly ignoring inflationary concerns. Oil has declined more than 10% now, which does provide a disinflationary vector at least. The rule of thumb we use is that -10% in oil leads to a -0.4% vector on the CPI. It's meaningfully helpful. On the other hand, the most damning leading indicator for inflation is expectations, collected in surveys by Michigan University, and they point to a continued rise in inflation expectations to 5%, which is the same as during the worst of the Ukraine war supply chain disruptions. Therefore, we are not betting on long-duration, low-risk credit either in the hopes of meaningful cuts, even though anything is possible at this stage.

Bottom Line

We don't have the duration disclosures for BIZD or constituent parts like ARCC, but with higher risk credit like this, there are also other factors in seeing sensitivity, particularly with credit spreads which also reflect recovery and bankruptcy conditions in a proportion of higher risk portfolios. What we can say though is that a 100-200 bps re-rating in credit spreads could realistically double the declines so far experienced, meaning another 15-20% downside, assuming we are only halfway to more accurate Ukraine war levels for credit spreads, taking our cue from B rated bonds. If a trade war turns out to be more economically costly for the US, we do think that the decline could be considerably ahead of 20%. BIZD is a macro-sensitive vehicle, and we'd stay out of the way of possible danger here.

data.title: TBIL: T-Bills And Chill?
data.description:
It has been a while since I last wrote about the US Treasury 3 Month Bill ETF (NASDAQ:TBIL). However, in the second half of last year, I wrote an article on a similar fund, the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) which argued investors should allocate some capital to treasury bills because they offered investors a "real option". For example, while TBIL's 4-5% returns were modest, they give investors the flexibility to redeploy capital into more attractive assets in the future if asset prices were to decline.

Brief Fund Overview

For those not familiar, the TBIL ETF is a very simple fund. Its holdings only comprise U.S. treasury bills that are rolled monthly, so it is one of the safest investments in the market, with zero credit risk and interest rate risk (Figure 1).

The TBIL ETF has nearly $5.4 billion in assets under management ("AUM") and charges investors a 0.15% expense ratio (Figure 2).

A Safe Haven In Stormy Seas

TBIL's flexibility is becoming increasingly important by the day, as President Trump's tariff plans announced last week threw global markets into turmoil (Figure 3).

Since Trump announced the incredibly high 'reciprocal tariffs' on nearly every country in the world, global markets like China, Europe, and Japan have been severely punished (Figure 4).

Even U.S. markets were not spared, as the S&P 500 Index has declined by a cumulative 11% in the past 3 trading sessions. Amidst this turmoil, treasury bills have continued to perform, with the TBIL ETF delivering a modest 1.0% in the last quarter and 2.2% in the past 6 months with no volatility and no fuss (Figure 5).

Expect Forward Returns To Decline As Fed May Be Forced To Cut Rates

However, looking forward, I fear the forward returns investors can expect from treasury bills and the TBIL will be dramatically lower, especially if President Trump goes through with his 'reciprocal tariff' plans on April 9th.

The main issue for treasury bills and the TBIL ETF is that short-term treasury bill yields are highly correlated with the Federal Reserve's Fed Funds rates (Figure 6). In effect, the Federal Reserve's monetary policies dictate short-term interest rates.

So if President Trump's tariffs cause the U.S. economy to weaken or fall into a recession (odds of a U.S. recession have surged in recent days to ~60%), then investors should expect the Federal Reserve to cut Fed Funds rates in order to support employment and the economy.

Already, President Trump has expressed his desire for the Fed to cut interest rates to dampen the negative impacts of his tariffs (Figure 7).

Likewise, fixed-income investor expectations for Fed rate cuts have surged in recent days, with Fed funds futures now pricing in a total of 4 rate cuts by year-end (Figure 8).

But Focus On Return Of Capital

However, a potential recessionary environment actually argues for a higher allocation to cash-like investments like the TBIL ETF. This is because risky assets like stocks, as represented by the S&P 500 Index, typically perform poorly when the economy heads into recession (Figure 9).

If the economy is heading into a recession, as some real-time indicators like the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow indicator suggest (Figure 10), investors should be more concerned about 'return of capital' rather than 'return on capital'.

Equity Valuations Improving

On the other hand, the risk of allocating too heavily to cash-like assets like the TBIL ETF is that investors may forgo potential returns on risky assets like stocks.

For value-oriented investors, I have argued for the past several months that equity valuations were not attractive as the S&P 500 Index was trading north of 21x Forward P/E. However, after the recent stock rout, market valuations are becoming more reasonable, with the S&P 500 Index now trading at 18.1x Fwd P/E and mid-cap stocks trading at 13.1x Fwd P/E (Figure 11).

At some point, I believe it will be prudent for value-oriented investors to increase their allocations to risky assets like stocks. However, as I have argued in a recent article on the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), we may need to see S&P 500 valuations decline to ~16x Fwd P/E (the level stocks bottomed in 2022) with assurances that the economy is not heading into a recession and thus earnings forecasts are reliable.

Conclusion

With President Trump launching 'reciprocal tariffs' on almost every country in the world, global markets have been thrown into chaos and stocks have plunged in the last few days.

If Trump's tariffs are implemented with the tariff rates announced, the U.S. may be introducing the largest tax hike since 1942, worth an estimated 2.9% of GDP. This could push the U.S. economy into a recession and may cause risky assets to fall.

In this uncertain environment, I believe it is prudent for value-oriented investors to preserve capital and increase their allocation to cash-like investments like the TBIL ETF. I am raising my recommendation on the TBIL ETF to buy.

data.title: First Financial Corporation: The Picture Is Improving
data.description:
One bank that has recently outperformed my expectations has been First Financial Corporation (NASDAQ:THFF). If this name is not familiar to you, that's understandable. After all, with a market capitalization of around $582 million, and operations based out of Indiana, First Financial Corporation is not exactly a household name. Well, back in early November of last year, I decided to dig into the business. I considered it to be an interesting firm, but not one that was worthy of a bullish assessment. At that time, I called it a ‘middle-of-the-pack’ prospect because of mixed financial results leading up to that point, with profitability being a particular issue. This ultimately led me to rate the company a ‘hold’.

From that time through today, First Financial Corporation has achieved some pretty solid financial results. Thanks to an increase in the size of its balance sheet and improvements when it came to its net interest margin, the company's revenue and profits improved year over year. For some investors, this might be enough to justify an upgrade. But I'm not there just yet. While the company does have impressive asset quality, especially compared to other similar firms, it's trading a bit higher than I would like it to. I will say that I am more optimistic about it now than I was late last year. But we will have to see more progress and either shares fall or fundamentals improve before I upgrade it.

Staying The Course

The most recent fundamental data that we have from First Financial Corporation covers the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. For that window of time, net interest income for the institution was $47.6 million. This represents an improvement over the $37.8 million the business reported just one year earlier. As you will see later in this article, the firm did enjoy growth from a balance sheet perspective. That's certainly helped. However, the enterprise also enjoyed a rise in its net interest margin from 3.63% to 3.94%. For the year as a whole, net interest income was still down, having contracted from $160 million to $158.8 million. A decline in the firm's net interest margin from 3.78% to 3.71% didn't help in this regard. Year over year, the business saw a surge in the provision for credit losses from $7.3 million to $16.2 million. Had it not been for this, net interest income would have actually risen year over year.

We also have seen some growth when it comes to non-interest income. For the final quarter of the year, it hit $12.2 million. That's a respectable improvement over the $11.2 million reported the previous year. For the year as a whole, we did see an increase also, but only barely from $42.7 million to $42.8 million. Unfortunately, improvements in key areas such as service charges and fees on deposit accounts, trust and financial services revenue, and loan servicing fees, were offset largely by a decline in other income from $5.9 million to $3.5 million. For the year as a whole, the weakness in net interest income, combined with a surge in non-interest expense led by higher salaries and employee benefits costs, higher equipment expenses, and higher other miscellaneous expenses, caused net profits to contract from $60.7 million to $47.3 million. However, for the final quarter of the year, the $16.2 million in profit generated by the company handily surpassed the $12.4 million reported the previous year.

When it comes to the balance sheet, we have seen some nice growth. Total deposits at the end of 2024, for instance, came out to $4.72 billion. That compares to the $4.09 billion the company had at the end of 2023 and is up from the $4.37 billion reported the previous year. A larger amount of deposits makes possible a larger amount of loans, all else being the same. And sure enough, those have increased as well. By the end of 2023, loans totaled $3.13 billion. But by the end of 2024, they had increased further to $3.79 billion. The largest chunk of these, approximately 57.3%, involved commercial loans. Another 25.2% involved residential loans, while the rest involved consumer for loans.

Obviously, there are other aspects of the balance sheet that deserve attention as well. Securities would be one such example. After all, this is a major part of the balance sheet of many financial firms, especially banks. Securities offer a way to diversify assets and to sometimes invest in assets that could generate greater upside. By the end of 2024, First Financial Corporation had $1.20 billion that fell under this category. Unfortunately, that was down from the $1.26 billion reported the previous year. It is worth noting that all of the company’s securities are classified as available for sale. This means that the company doesn't have the intention of holding these long term. So it's not shocking to see big changes. In fact, for the past few years now, First Financial Corporation has been reducing its securities exposure. At the end of 2022, for instance, they totaled $1.33 billion.

Over the same window of time from 2023 to 2024, the value of cash and cash equivalents grew from $77 million to $93.5 million. Meanwhile, the value of debt on its books rose from $175.8 million to $215.2 million. Considering the size of the business, I don't consider these numbers to be all that material. Furthermore, while I would prefer to see cash increase and debt decrease, these changes are not significant enough to affect my overall assessment of the company more broadly.

One thing that I really don't like about First Financial Corporation is its valuation. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced on a price to earnings basis. Even though it's near the low end of the range, with one of the five companies I compared it to in that chart trading cheaper than it is, the fact that its price to earnings multiple is 11.0 is still disappointing. In this space, I typically like to see a price to earnings multiple of between 6 and 10. The picture does look a bit better when it comes to both the price to book approach and the price to tangible book approach. In the chart below, I compared it to the same five businesses using these multiples. My strong preference would be to see the company trade at or below book value. But that's not terribly common these days. It does meet that standard on a price to book basis, but not on a price to tangible book basis. One of the five companies I compared it to are cheaper than it is on a price to book basis. And when it comes to the price to tangible book approach, only two of them are cheaper.

Even though the stock is not as cheap as I would like it to be, it is important to note that this is compensated to some extent by its asset quality. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets when stacked against the same five businesses I have compared it to already. With a reading of 1.18%, it stands above the other five enterprises. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see the same type of comparative analysis using return on equity. In this case, the 11.68% reading that we get from First Financial Corporation places it higher than four of the five companies I compared it to. This certainly deserves some optimism. But it's not enough for a value oriented investor like myself to see in order to push up.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I am currently more optimistic about First Financial Corporation than I was late last year. The most important aspects of its balance sheet continue to grow, and recent income statement data was encouraging. Asset quality remains high, and the stock is a little bit on the cheaper end of the spectrum compared to other similar enterprises. However, it's not quite cheap enough for somebody who wants market beating returns, especially in light of broader economic concerns. Because of this, I have decided to keep the company rated a ‘hold’ for now.
